schools.summary: ‘True’ (alēthinē) = the genuine vine of which the natural is a figure (suggested by the Supper’s ‘fruit of the vine,’ the temple’s golden vine, or a vineyard en route); the true vine against Israel the degenerate vine (Ps 80; Isa 5; Jer 2).
Augustine/Hilary: Christ the vine = Head of the Church, of one nature with the branches as man; he became man that human nature might be the vine and we its branches.
Calvin: by nature we are barren and dry except as engrafted into Christ, drawing from him a new power not our own.

The husbandman’s two works: he ‘takes away’ the fruitless ‘branch in me’ (professing but lifeless Christians — Judas, nominal believers) and ‘purges’ the fruitful for more fruit (by truth and suffering, 1 Pet 1:22; Heb 12:10).
Calvin: the fruitless are those ‘supposed to be in Christ’ by profession but with ‘no root’ — not true members cut off; the fruitful are pruned, not rejected, for more fruit.

debates.question: Who are the fruitless branches ‘in me’ that are taken away?

sides.position: merely professing/nominal believers who never had vital union
those once attached by faith in his word who fall away (Judas, apostates)

schools.summary: ‘Clean’ (= ‘pruned,’ keeping the figure) by Christ’s whole word (not just 13:10); ‘already clean,’ the disciples purified through his teaching (perhaps alluding to the uncircumcised-fruit law, Lev 19:23).
Calvin/Wesley: the word (outward preaching) is the instrument of cleansing, made effectual by the Spirit working in the heart.

‘Abide in me, and I in you’ — continuing union by faith; as the branch bears nothing ‘of itself’ apart from the vine, so the disciple bears no fruit except by abiding; cleansing is his gift, abiding their duty (Chrysostom).
Augustine: the two abidings differ — the branch contributes nothing to the vine but receives all; both are for the disciples’ profit, not Christ’s.

themes: ‘abide in me, and I in you’

schools.summary: The abiding union brings ‘much fruit’; but ‘without (apart from) me ye can do nothing’ — severed from the vine the branch is wholly fruitless; all spiritual fruit is Christ’s life through the disciple.
Calvin: total dependence — the death-blow to self-reliance; whatever natural good the world does, in God’s things the cut branch can do nothing.
The branch that does not abide is cast out, withers, is gathered and burned — the end of a fruitless profession (apostates, false professors); a solemn warning to examine whether one truly abides.
Calvin: those who ‘seem engrafted’ yet have no root — a warning against false security; the only safety is abiding in the vine.

Abiding is defined as Christ’s words abiding in them (the means and proof of union); the astounding ‘ask what ye will’ presupposes such harmony with Christ that nothing is asked out of accord with his mind.
Calvin: a remedy for the believer’s felt poverty — God trains us to earnest prayer by hunger; Wesley: prayer is a fruit of faith that produces more fruit.

themes: ‘if ye abide… ask what ye will’

schools.summary: Much fruit glorifies the Father (showing the law’s excellence, the gospel’s power, the image restored) and proves true discipleship — ‘so shall ye become my disciples.’

Chrysostom: since their fruit is the Father’s glory, he will not despise his glory but keep them fruitful; Augustine: a commendation of saving grace; Calvin: God hears prayers for fruitfulness, which serves his glory.
The chain of love: as the Father loved the Son (the highest love), so Christ loved his own; ‘continue in my love’ — our love for him the result of his for us, grounded in the Father’s love (3:16).
Calvin: not an abstruse inquiry into love within the Godhead but ‘a sure pledge of God’s love towards us’; Wesley: keep your place in his affection, do not forfeit it.

Keeping the commandments is the proof and condition of abiding in Christ’s love (correlative of 14:15); not the cause of his love but the way it is enjoyed; Christ himself the example, abiding in the Father’s love by obedience.
Calvin: the method of perseverance — to follow where he calls; faith and a good conscience are inseparable (Rom 8:1); he does not reconcile us to indulge in sin.
The purpose of the discourse: that Christ’s own joy might ‘remain’ in them and their joy be ‘full’ — all spoken to gladden, not burden, the disciples.
Chrysostom: the joy must be carried to the end — like a ship that must reach harbour; those who fall back near the end lose it; the fullness of joy is for the persevering.
The new commandment (13:34) made central — ‘love one another, as I have loved you’; the standard is Christ’s own self-giving love, the fruit and proof of abiding in his love (v.9).
Augustine: the same ‘new commandment’ — the love commanded is the very love with which Christ loved us, its pattern and power.

themes: ‘love one another, as I have loved you’

schools.summary: The highest conceivable love — laying down life for friends (cf. the good shepherd, 10:11; 1 John 3:16); Christ’s own love, about to be shown on the cross, is the measure of the love commanded.
Augustine: the fullness of love we owe one another; Calvin: he proclaims his love both to confirm our salvation and to inflame us, by his example, to brotherly love.
The dignity of being Christ’s ‘friends’ (all who love and obey him, not the apostles only), marked by continued obedience — a prerequisite of fellowship (Mt 28:20).
Augustine: great condescension — the obedience that proves good servants he makes the mark of friends; Calvin: not merit but the condition of favour.
Wesley: ‘on this condition, not otherwise — a thunderbolt for Antinomianism.’
No longer servants (who know not the lord’s doings) but friends — marked by the intimacy of revelation: Christ has made known what he heard from the Father (cf. Abraham, Gen 18:17).
Augustine: ‘all things’ = what was needful and fitting; Origen: the contrast of the spirit of bondage and the freedom of friends; Calvin: opening his mind is a pledge of his love.
Reversing custom (the disciple chose his master), Christ chose and appointed them (the word of ‘election,’ 6:70; Lk 6:13) — that they should go, bear lasting fruit, and have prayer answered.
Calvin: their honour is of grace, not merit — ‘ye have not chosen me but I have chosen you’ claims the whole calling for Christ alone, against any concurrence of grace and human will.
The central command repeated as the sum of all that precedes — the vine, abiding, choosing, friendship all converge on ‘love one another.’
Augustine: love is the fruit Christ appointed (the fruit of the Spirit, Gal 5:22), commended ‘as if the only thing commanded,’ for without it nothing profits.

The turn from brotherly love to the world’s hatred: Christ forewarns and comforts — it ‘hated me before it hated you,’ so persecution is no strange thing but the world’s old enmity, first to the Master.
Calvin: a needed forewarning lest the disciples be shocked; to be hated by the world is to share Christ’s lot, the badge of union with the hated Lord.
The world loves its own; it hates the disciples because they are ‘not of the world’ — chosen out of it, their holiness a reproach to the sinful world (7:7; 1 John 3:13).
Calvin: the hatred is a consolation — it springs from their being separated from the world by grace, the badge of their rescue from destruction.
‘The servant is not greater than his lord’ (13:16; Mt 10:24): the disciples will be persecuted as Christ was (5:16), but some will keep their word as some kept his.
Augustine: Christ is the supreme example of patient endurance (1 Pet 2:21), done by his help; Calvin: the servant’s condition cannot be better than the Master’s (confirming v.18).

themes: ‘the servant is not greater than his lord’

schools.summary: They suffer ‘for my name’s sake’ — not as evildoers but as Christ’s representatives (Mt 10:22; the Acts a commentary); the root is ignorance of God, ‘the foundation of all religious persecution.’
Calvin: the world’s mad enmity against its own salvation springs from blind ignorance hurrying it to destruction; Wesley: in all ages those who know not God hate those who do.

Not sinlessness before Christ, but the peculiar, inexcusable sin of rejecting the Messiah in the face of his word (and works) — apart from which their sin would have belonged to the overlooked ‘times of ignorance’ (Acts 17:30).
Augustine/Chrysostom: the sin of unbelief despite his doctrine and miracles; Calvin: their ignorance is no excuse — it is willful, malicious blindness.
Since Christ reveals God (14:9), to hate him is to hate the Father (5:23) — the successive degrees of sin: hating the disciples, the Son, the Father; ‘no greater darkness.’
Augustine: they hate the true God under their false notion of him, refusing the God revealed in Christ; Gill: the hatred arises from the heart’s natural enmity to God.

themes: ‘he that hateth me hateth my Father’
hatred of Christ is hatred of God

schools.summary: The works (‘which none other man did’) added to the words leave them inexcusable; having ‘seen’ the works that reveal the Father in the Son, they hated both — guilt full where revelation is full.
Augustine: none healed so many as Christ; the strangeness of hating what is seen to be divine; Calvin: seeing aggravates the guilt — they hated against the clearest light.

themes: ‘the works which none other man did’

schools.summary: The causeless hatred fulfils Scripture (Ps 69:4; 35:19; ‘law’ = whole Scripture, 10:34); ‘without a cause’ (dōrean) — Christ broke no law yet was hated, as sinners still hate him.
Calvin: nothing is more against reason than to hate God; the citation reproves the nation’s obstinate malice continued ‘from age to age’; the hatred is part of God’s mysterious purpose.

The answer to the disciples’ fear of a hostile world: the Spirit of truth, sent by the Son from the Father and proceeding from the Father, ‘shall testify of me’ — the principal witness (over the apostles’ subordinate one, v.27).
Calvin: the Spirit’s testimony is the disciples’ unshakable stay against the world; Augustine: the procession of the Spirit.
Wesley: the Spirit’s coming, sending, and title mark him a distinct Divine person, proceeding from the Father (and arguably the Son).

themes: the Spirit shall testify of Christ

schools.summary: The apostles’ subordinate, eyewitness testimony joined to the Spirit’s — qualified by having been ‘with me from the beginning’ (Mt 4:17; Acts 1:21-22; 1 John 1:1), distinct from yet united with the Spirit’s witness.
Calvin and others: the Spirit and the apostles are the double foundation of the church’s witness — the Spirit makes the eyewitness testimony effectual.
